statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers `arr`, a subarray is considered *nice* if the product of its elements is a perfect square. You are allowed to swap elements in the subarray at most once (with an exception that you can swap an element with itself). Return the length of the longest nice subarray.

A perfect square is an integer equal to the square of an integer, i.e., `n == i * i` where `i` is an integer.

examples.explanation: The entire array is a nice subarray because the product of all the elements is 120, which is not a perfect square, this is incorrect so lets assume 4  is swapped with 1 then product is 24 which is also not a perfect square. lets try 4 with 2 then we get 3 * 5 * 2 * 1 = 60 which is also not perfect square. But 4 * 1 = 4 which is perfect square. So subarray [1 4] or [4 1] both are nice subarray. And also [1 2 3 4] can be made nice by swapping 2 and 3 then product is 720 and 720 = 12 * 12 * 5 which is not a perfect square. But if we take [3 4] and swap 4 with 3 then 3 * 3 = 9 which is a perfect square. So subarray [3 4] or [4 3] are nice subarray. So maximum length of nice subarray is 2.
Subarray [1,4] can be made nice by swapping 1 and 4.

hints.text: To solve this problem, you need to consider the prime factorization of the numbers in the array, specifically the exponents of their prime factors.
A perfect square has all even exponents in its prime factorization. You can utilize this property to identify nice subarrays.
You can use a hashmap or similar data structure to track the prime factorization of the products of subarrays and their corresponding indices.
The problem allows at most one swap in the subarray. This means you need to consider how a single swap can make an odd exponent even, or vice versa.

The core technique here involves using a sliding window approach combined with tracking the parity of exponents of prime factors within the window, allowing for a single swap that could make all exponents even.

editorial.title: Optimizing the Count of Nice Subarrays
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the maximum length of a subarray where all numbers have prime factors with even counts, except for the fact that we can have at most one odd count for each prime factor across the subarray. A brute force approach would be inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subarrays and checking if each subarray meets the condition. This would involve a time complexity of O(n^2 * m * log(m)) where n is the length of the array and m is the maximum value in the array. This approach is correct but slow.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a sliding window technique along with tracking the prime factors of each number. By maintaining a count of odd factors for each prime factor within the current window, we can efficiently determine if the current subarray meets the condition.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided Python solution uses this approach, achieving a time complexity of O(n*m*log(m)) and a space complexity of O(m). It iterates over the array, expanding the window to the right and updating the count of odd factors for each prime factor. When the condition is met, it updates the maximum length of the subarray.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it correctly counts the odd factors for each prime factor within the window and updates the maximum length accordingly. The use of a sliding window technique allows for efficient exploration of all possible subarrays.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs can help verify its correctness. For instance, an array with a single element that is a perfect square should return 1. An array with multiple elements that are perfect squares should return the length of the subarray containing all perfect squares.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly counting the odd factors for each prime factor
Failing to update the maximum length of the subarray when the condition is met
Not using a sliding window technique to efficiently explore all possible subarrays
Incorrectly handling the case where a number has multiple prime factors with odd counts
Not considering the space complexity of the solution
Incorrectly assuming the time complexity of the brute force approach
